Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely happened that way. I'd often end up cramming things in whenever I could find the energy, which wasn't always consistent. Being 27 and juggling other life stuff made it hard to stick to any real schedule. I'd sometimes have bursts of productivity followed by days where I just couldn't bring myself to open anything course-related.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd be relatively consistent, maybe studying in the evenings after work or other commitments. But then deadlines would pile up and I'd shift into panic mode, studying at odd hours. The stress would build and I'd either push through or completely shut down for a bit.
Q3: Usually it was fear, to be honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered something was due or when the anxiety got bad enough that I had to check what I was missing. Sometimes a peer would message me asking about an assignment and that would prompt me to go look. It wasn't really a proactive thing—more reactive, driven by that scared feeling in my gut.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic or natural. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every morning, but that fell apart quickly. Life got in the way, my anxiety spiked, and suddenly it felt overwhelming to even open the platform. The routine never stuck.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out in text form that I could download and annotate. Discussion posts were sometimes helpful too, especially when peers explained concepts in different ways. The peer support I got connected with actually made some of the content click better.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos, honestly. Watching 75 minutes of video content felt like a lot when I could skim through written materials faster. I also delayed discussion posts until the last minute because putting my thoughts out there made me anxious. Some of the optional supplementary materials I just never touched—there was already too much required stuff I was barely keeping up with.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to passing the course or understanding something I was confused about. If something felt like busywork or wasn't obviously linked to assessments, I'd deprioritize it. I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent on things that actually mattered for my grade.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With my attendance already spotty and assignments piling up, anything labeled optional immediately went to the bottom of my list. Required things had this weight of consequence attached to them—skip them and you fail. Optional stuff? My anxious brain said 'you're already drowning, don't add more.'
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded PDFs and readings constantly and studied them offline on my tablet or printed them out. I have notebooks full of handwritten notes that the LMS would never know about. I also used external websites and YouTube videos to understand concepts when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through downloaded materials, rewriting notes, or talking through concepts with friends who weren't even in the course. I'd be genuinely engaging with the content but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days. My peer support group met up in person sometimes, and those conversations helped me understand things better than any online activity could show.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it immediately, and then never go back to that page on the LMS. I preferred having everything saved locally where I could access it without internet or without the anxiety of seeing all my incomplete tasks staring at me. The LMS interface itself stressed me out, so I avoided it when I could.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was scared I was going to fail, and everything felt insurmountable. There were weeks where just thinking about the course made my chest tight. Work pressures and personal stuff compounded everything. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me back from completely checking out, but there were definitely dark periods.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Competing responsibilities meant I'd have to choose between course stuff and other urgent life things. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them because I didn't know where to begin. Fatigue made even simple tasks feel monumental.
Q14: Many times. I'd scroll through materials, technically 'accessing' them, but my brain was elsewhere—worrying about other things or just too tired to absorb anything. I'd have readings open while my mind raced with anxiety about completely unrelated stuff. The system would log my activity but I wasn't really there, you know?
Q15: All the time. I'd avoid discussion posts because putting my thoughts out there felt exposing and scary. I'd postpone assignments even when I knew exactly what needed to be done because starting felt overwhelming. It's like my anxiety created this paralysis—the more important something was, the more terrified I was of messing it up, so I'd avoid it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation—or really, the panic—to engage. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered right before due dates. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I survived the course.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more, at least temporarily. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid the course for a while. I needed time to process criticism before I could productively move forward.
Q18: The structure felt loose in ways that didn't help someone like me who struggles with self-direction when anxious. I needed more scaffolding, more check-ins, more structure to keep me on track. When things were too open-ended, I'd flounder. The peer support component was actually the most helpful structural element because it gave me human accountability.
Q19: Having resources available for download made things easier—I could study on my own terms. But the sheer volume of materials was overwhelming, and I often didn't know what to prioritize. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped. The discussion requirements added pressure that sometimes backfired for my anxiety.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my anxious days—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but being too overwhelmed to actually complete anything. You might be reading, watching, attempting to understand, but the actual submission feels too scary or you run out of time. Activity doesn't equal output when you're struggling.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in my activity logs. Someone could download everything early, study intensively away from the platform, and only return to submit work. The LMS only captures one slice of how someone engages with course material. My decent assignment scores came from offline effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you show up. But they miss the quality of that engagement, the emotional state behind it, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture fear, determination, or the conversations with peers that actually helped concepts stick. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when you're curious, when you think about course concepts outside of study time, when you want to discuss ideas with others. For me, real engagement happened in spurts—moments of genuine interest between long stretches of just trying to survive.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to me, or the genuine learning that happened in our conversations. It wouldn't capture the nights I spent anxiously reviewing downloaded materials, or the relief when something finally clicked. It can't show that despite my spotty attendance and low engagement scores, I actually cared—I was just scared and struggling to show up consistently.
